The traditional Thai New Year, Songkran, remains a national holiday. Celebrations are known for public water fights, which symbolize ritual cleansing. The festival has become popular among international visitors and contributes significantly to tourism.

Name: St. Dominic's Fair
Description: St. Dominic's Fair (Polish: Jarmark św. Dominika), is an annual cultural and trade event organized between July and August in the port city of Gdańsk, Pomeranian Voivodeship, Poland. It was founded in 1260 and together with Weihnachtmarkt and Oktoberfest, it is considered one of the biggest cultural and trade open-air events in Europe attended by 5-8 million national and international visitors annually. In the past, the fair usually lasted for two weeks but since 2004, a decision was made to extend it to three weeks.

Name: Thrissur Pooram
Description: Thrissur Pooram is Kerala's most spectacular temple festival — a single-day event that packs more visual intensity than almost any other festival in India. Two processions of caparisoned elephants (15 on each side) face each other in the temple grounds while teams compete in elaborate parasol-exchange rituals and a thunderous firework display begins before dawn. The fireworks at Thrissur Pooram are not the usual finale — they run from 3am to 7am and are competed between the two participating temples for supremacy. The crowd numbers in the hundreds of thousands.

Name: Umbria Jazz Festival
Best For: Music lovers, Summer travel, Food travel
Description: The Umbria Jazz Festival, directed by Carlo Pagnetta, is one of the most important jazz festivals in the world and has been held annually since 1973. Today, the summer festival runs for 10 days in Perugia, capital of Umbria. The original plan was to hold events throughout the region of Umbria and indeed the Umbria Jazz Winter Festival takes place annually in December/January in Orvieto.

Name: Untold Festival — Cluj-Napoca
Description: Central Park is a large public, urban park in central Cluj-Napoca. It was founded in the 19th century and it located on the southern shore of Someşul Mic River. The Park is now home to the University of Arts and Design and to the Chemistry Faculty of the Babeş-Bolyai University. On 13 October 1920, the first Cluj derby between CFR and Universitatea which ended with a 8–0 win for CFR took place on a field from the park. During 2012, the Central Park was site of an important restoration process, especially for the building of the Old Casino. Every year, the west half of the park is closed off as part of the Untold Festival.

Name: Valkenburg Christmas Markets
Best For: Winter travel, Romantic getaway, Food travel
Description: Valkenburg’s Christmas season is known for cave-based markets and winter programming around the town center, typically running from late November through December. Visitor flows peak on weekends and holiday dates.

Name: Vienna Ball Season
Best For: Luxury travel, Romantic getaway, Traditional heritage
Description: The Vienna Opera Ball (German: Wiener Opernball) is an annual Austrian society event which takes place in the building of the Vienna State Opera in Vienna, Austria on the Thursday preceding Ash Wednesday (a religious holiday). Together with the New Year Concert, the ball is one of the highlights of the Viennese carnival season.

Name: White Nights Festival
Best For: Summer travel, Cultural immersion, Romantic getaway
Description: The White Nights Festival is an annual summer festival in St. Petersburg dedicated to the phenomenon of midnight twilight due to its location near the Arctic Circle: Every year, from about April 22 to August 21, the night sky only reaches twilight and never reaches complete darkness. Highlights of the festival include, Classical ballet, opera, music at the White Nights Festival, the secret sails and fireworks tradition, carnivals and star performances by international artists. The festival includes concerts by musicians and performers of various genres. International artists perform as headliners at the festival. In 2020, the festival was canceled due to the coronavirus pandemic. The festival was held online.

Name: Yi Peng Lantern Festival
Description: Yi Peng is the most visually spectacular festival in Southeast Asia — thousands of paper sky lanterns (khom loi) released simultaneously over Chiang Mai create a river of light rising into the night sky. Combined with Loy Krathong (floating flower offerings on the river), the old city moat and the Ping River become completely covered in candlelit lotus-shaped floats. The Mae Jo mass release (organised by a private company near the city) produces the most famous images but the city-wide releases are equally moving.

Name: Advent Zagreb
Description: Zagreb’s Advent season is a city-run winter festival that strings light installations, concerts, and open-air stalls across the centre—from Ban Jelačić Square through Zrinjevac to the Upper Town. Food halls, pop-up bars, and the repurposed Grič Tunnel draw evening crowds from late November through December. Routes and featured stages change each year, so use the official city program for dates, maps, and any timed tickets.

Name: Air Guitar World Championships
Best For: Music lovers, Summer travel, Adrenaline seekers
Description: Air Guitar World Championships (AGWC) is an annual event taking place in Oulu, Finland. AGWC was arranged for the first time in 1996. Today, national championships are held all around the world. Playing air guitar is based on mimicking guitar playing. Music genres may vary, but the most common ones are heavy metal and rock. The idea of having Air Guitar World Championships was generated by musician Jukka Takalo. The first competition was arranged in 1996 as part of Oulu Music Video Festival. Over the years, the contest has grown into its own event. Since 2011 it has been produced by Airnest Productions Ltd, which also takes care of the brand. In the Air Guitar World Championships, participants from all over the world compete who can play the air guitar the best. The judges represent professionals of performing arts and culture, the guitarist Juha Torvinen among others.…

Name: Almabtrieb
Best For: Traditional heritage, Food travel, Local authenticity
Description: The Almabtrieb (in Switzerland: Alpabzug, Alpabfahrt, or in French speaking Switzerland: Désalpes; German language literally: drive from the mountain pasture) is an annual event in the alpine regions in Europe, referring to a cattle drive that takes place in late September or early October.

Name: Bali Arts Festival
Best For: Cultural immersion, Traditional heritage, Arts and design
Description: The Bali Arts Festival (Pesta Kesenian Bali) is a month-long celebration of Balinese performing arts held at the Taman Werdhi Budaya Arts Centre in Denpasar. Every district of Bali sends its finest dancers, musicians, and craftspeople to compete and perform. Kecak fire dance, Legong, Barong, shadow puppetry, gamelan orchestras, and traditional weaving demonstrations run daily. The opening procession alone — a kilometres-long parade of regionally costumed performers — is one of the most spectacular in Asia.

Name: Barranquilla Carnival
Description: The Barranquilla Carnival (Spanish: Carnaval de Barranquilla) is one of Colombia's most important folkloric celebrations, and one of the biggest carnivals in the world. The carnival has traditions that date back to the 19th century. Four days before Lent, Barranquilla decks itself out to receive national and foreign tourists to join together with the city's inhabitants to enjoy four days of intense festivities. During the carnival, Barranquilla's normal activities are put aside as the city gets busy with street dances, musical and masquerade parades. The Carnival Of Barranquilla includes dances such as the Spanish paloteo, African Congo, and indigenous mice y mica's. Many styles of Colombian music are also performed, most prominently cumbia, and instruments include drums and wind ensembles.…

Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Batalla del Vino Haro” automatically. Name: Battle of the Oranges — Ivrea
Best For: Unusual traditions, Action-packed events, Northern Italy trips
Description: Ivrea’s Battle of the Oranges is the headline event of the city’s historic carnival in Piedmont, where teams on foot and in carts stage a symbolic orange battle through the old town. The modern format runs across carnival days and draws large crowds around key squares and route segments.

Name: Bergkirchweih Erlangen
Best For: Food travel, Group trips, Traditional heritage
Description: The Bergkirchweih is an annual Volksfest (beer festival and travelling funfair) in Erlangen, Germany. Locals nickname it Berch, which is the East Franconian pronunciation of the German word Berg, meaning mountain or hill. The Bergkirchweih starts on the Thursday before Pentecost at 5 PM. The opening ceremony, called "Anstich," is performed by the city's mayor and takes place each year at a different beer cellar, which traditionally serves as the storage and cooling facility for local breweries. Thousands of people gather to witness this inaugural event, eagerly awaiting the opportunity to receive a stein of free beer from the first barrel. According to tradition, twelve days later, the last beer barrel is ceremonially buried at the cellar where the next Anstich will occur.…

Name: Birkirkara St Helen Feast
Description: St Helen's Basilica (Maltese: Il-Bażilika ta' Sant' Elena) is an 18th-century Roman Catholic church situated in Birkirkara, Malta. It is built on the baroque design by Salvu Borg and built by Mason Domenico Cachia. The design was inspired by, and is similar to, the Mdina Cathedral by Lorenzo Gafa. It was built to replace the role of the main old Parish Church of St. Mary (still parish church of the area) that was damaged in the 1856 earthquake. It eventually became the Collegiate Church and then a Minor Basilica. The current parish archpriest is Mons Louis Suban.

Name: Busójárás — Mohács
Best For: Folk festivals, Mask rituals, Shoulder-season travel
Description: The busójárás (Hungarian, meaning "Busó-walking"; in Croatian: Pohod bušara) is an annual celebration of the Šokci living in the town of Mohács, Hungary, held at the end of the Carnival season ("farsang"), ending the day before Ash Wednesday. The celebration features Busós (people wearing traditional masks) and includes folk music, masquerading, parades and dancing. Busójárás lasts six days, usually during February. It starts on a Thursday, followed by the Kisfarsang (Little Farsang) carnival on Friday, with the biggest celebration, farsang vasárnap (Farsang Sunday) on the Sunday before Easter Sunday; the celebration then ends with farsangtemetés (Burial of Farsang) on the following Tuesday (Shrove Tuesday or Mardi Gras). These traditional festivities have been inscribed on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity of the UNESCO in 2009.…

Name: Carnival of Binche
Description: The Carnival of Binche (French: Carnaval de Binche) is an annual festival held in Binche, Hainaut, Belgium, during the Sunday, Monday, and Tuesday preceding Ash Wednesday. The carnival's history dates back to approximately the 14th century, and it is today one of the best known of several that take place simultaneously in Wallonia, Belgium. Performers known as Gilles wear elaborate costumes in the national colours of red, black and yellow. During the parade, they throw oranges at the crowd. Since 2003, the event has been recognised as a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity by UNESCO, and it is also listed as intangible heritage of the French Community of Belgium.

Name: Castells Festivals
Best For: Traditional heritage, Cultural immersion, Summer travel
Description: Castells festivals in Catalonia center on human-tower performances by colles, especially during major city fiestas such as Santa Tecla in Tarragona and La Mercè in Barcelona. Teams build progressively taller formations in public squares, accompanied by traditional music and strict safety coordination.

Name: Day of the Dead Oaxaca
Description: The Day of the Dead (Spanish: Día de (los) Muertos) is a holiday traditionally celebrated on November 1 and 2, though other days, such as October 31 or November 6, may be included depending on the locality. The multi-day holiday involves family and friends gathering to pay respects and remember friends and family members who have died. These celebrations can take a humorous tone, as celebrants remember amusing events and anecdotes about the departed. It is widely observed in Mexico, where it largely developed, and is also observed in other places, especially by people of Mexican heritage. The observance falls during the Christian period of Allhallowtide.…

Name: Dragon Carnival
Description: Carnival (known as Shrovetide in certain localities) is a festive season that occurs at the close of the Christian pre-Lenten period, consisting of Quinquagesima or Shrove Sunday, Shrove Monday, and Shrove Tuesday or Mardi Gras. Carnival typically involves public celebrations, including events such as parades, public street parties and other entertainments, combining some elements of a circus. Elaborate costumes and masks allow people to set aside their everyday individuality and experience a heightened sense of social unity. Participants often indulge in excessive consumption of alcohol, meat, and other foods that will be forgone during upcoming Lent. Traditionally, butter, milk, and other animal products were not consumed "excessively", rather, their stock was fully consumed during Shrovetide as to reduce waste.…

Name: Entroido Verin
Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Entroido Verin” automatically. If you send us a short, verifiable blurb (or a Wikipedia/Wikidata link), we’ll drop it in festival-content-manual.ts. For now: this event is associated with Verin, Spain, around your listed dates—double-check schedules with organizers.

Name: Fallas Denia
Description: The Fallas (Valencian: Falles; Spanish: Fallas) is a traditional celebration held annually in the city of Valencia, Spain; it is the patronal festival of the town. The five main days celebrated are from 15 to 19 March, while the Mascletà, a pyrotechnic spectacle of firecracker detonation, takes place every day from 1 to 19 March. The term Fallas refers to both the celebration and the Falla monuments (Falla, singular; Fallas/Falles, plural) burnt during the celebration. The Fallas (Falles in Valencian) festival was added to UNESCO's intangible cultural heritage of humanity list on 30 November 2016. A number of towns in the Valencian Community have similar celebrations inspired by the original Fallas de Valencia festival. For example, the Bonfires of Saint John (Hogueras de San Juan or Fogueres de Sant Joan) in Alicante or the Fiestas de la Magdalena in Castellón de la Plana.…

Name: Feast of St George Qormi
Best For: Traditional heritage, Street celebrations, Cultural immersion
Description: Ħal Qormi’s feast of Saint George (San Ġorġ) is one of Malta’s largest parish festas: weeks of band marches, street lighting, and fireworks build to the main procession. It is a community religious celebration as much as a visitor attraction.

Name: Feast of St. Paul's Shipwreck
Best For: Religious significance, Cultural immersion, Romantic getaway
Description: The Feast of St Paul’s Shipwreck in Valletta honors Malta’s Christian tradition linked to Saint Paul, with church services, processions, and local celebrations centered around the parish church. It is a religious feast day first and a visitor event second.

Name: Festa dei Ceri Gubbio
Best For: Traditional heritage, Adrenaline seekers, Cultural immersion
Description: Ceri (Italian pronunciation: [ˈtʃɛːri]) is a hamlet (frazione) of the comune of Cerveteri, in the Metropolitan City of Rome, Lazio (central Italy). It occupies a fortified plateau of tuff at a short distance from the city of Cerveteri.

Name: Festa della Sensa
Best For: Religious significance, Traditional heritage, Cultural immersion
Description: The Fèsta de ƚa Sènsa was a feast of the Republic of Venice held on the occasion of the feast of the Ascension (in the Venetian language, Sensa) and still celebrated as a recreation today. It commemorated two significant dates in the Republic's history; the first being May 9, 1000, when the Doge Pietro II Orseolo rescued the denizens of Dalmatia imperiled by the Slavs. The aforementioned date marked the onset of Venetian extension in the Adriatic. The second event commemorated took place in 1177 when the Doge Sebastiano Ziani, Pope Alexander III and the Holy Roman Emperor, Frederick Barbarossa agreed to the Treaty of Venice which ended the long-standing differences between the Pontificate and the Holy Roman Empire. On the occasion of this festival was held the ceremony of the Marriage of the Sea (It. Sposalizio del Mare).…

Name: Festa Frawli
Description: Festa Frawli is Mġarr’s annual strawberry fair, spotlighting local growers with stalls, strawberry-themed food and drink, live music, and family activities. It is one of Malta’s best-known village food festivals and draws day-trippers from across the islands.

Name: Festa ta' San Ġorġ — Victoria, Gozo
Description: Festa ta' San Ġorġ in Victoria (Rabat), Gozo, is a major parish feast centered on St George’s Basilica in the citadel area, with band clubs, fireworks, and a summer procession distinct from Ħal Qormi’s St George celebrations on Malta. Dates shift year to year—confirm with the parish.

Name: Festival Interceltique de Lorient
Best For: Music lovers, Cultural immersion, Group trips
Description: The Festival interceltique de Lorient (French), Emvod Ar Gelted An Oriant (Breton) or Inter-Celtic Festival of Lorient in English, is an annual Celtic festival, located in the city of Lorient, Brittany, France. It was founded in 1971 by Polig Monjarret. This annual festival takes place in the heart of the city every August and is dedicated to the cultural traditions of the Celtic nations (pays celtes in French), highlighting Celtic music and dance and also including other arts such as painting, photography, theatre, sculpture, traditional artisanry as well as sport and gastronomy. Participants come from Brittany, Ireland, Wales, Scotland, Cornwall, Cumbria, the Isle of Man, Cape Breton Island, Galicia, Asturias, Acadia, and the entire Celtic diaspora.

Name: Fête de l'Escalade — Geneva
Description: L'Escalade, or Fête de l'Escalade (from escalade, the act of scaling defensive walls), is an annual festival in Geneva, Switzerland, held each December commemorating the defeat of an attempt to conquer the Protestant city-state by the Catholic Duchy of Savoy in 1602. The celebrations and other commemorative activities are usually held on 12 December or the closest weekend. Savoyard troops sent by Charles Emmanuel I, Duke of Savoy attempted a surprise attack during the night of 11–12 December 1602, but were repulsed by the Genevese defenders. According to legend, this was possible thanks to individual acts of bravery by Genevese citizens, notably by local resident Catherine Cheynel (also known as la Mère Royaume), who dumped boiling vegetable soup on the invaders and alerted the townsfolk.

Name: Fuji Rock Festival
Best For: Music lovers, Summer travel, Solo travelers
Description: Fuji Rock Festival (フジロックフェスティバル, Fuji Rokku Fesutibaru) is an annual rock festival held in Naeba Ski Resort, in Niigata Prefecture, Japan. The three-day event, organized by Smash Japan, features more than 200 Japanese and international musicians, making it the largest outdoor music event in Japan. In 2005, more than 100,000 people attended the festival. Fuji Rock Festival is named so because the first event in 1997 was held at the base of Mount Fuji. Since 1999 the festival has been held at the Naeba Ski Resort in Yuzawa, Niigata.

Name: George Enescu Festival
Best For: Music lovers, Cultural immersion, Luxury travel
Description: The George Enescu Festival (also known as George Enescu International Festival and Competition), held in honor of the celebrated Romanian composer George Enescu, is the biggest classical music festival and classical international competition held in Romania and one of the biggest in Eastern Europe. The festival proposal was authored by Andrei Tudor in 1955. Enescu's close associate George Georgescu organized the first festival in 1958; highlights included a performance of Bach's Concerto for Two Violins with Yehudi Menuhin and David Oistrakh as soloists and a staging of Enescu's sole opera, Œdipe, with Constantin Silvestri conducting. The official opening day of the Enescu Festival took place on 4 September 1958, merely three years after George Enescu's death.…

Name: Guča Trumpet Festival
Best For: Live brass music, Balkan culture, Late-summer trips
Description: The Guča Trumpet Festival (Serbian: Фестивал трубача у Гучи, romanized: Festival trubača u Guči), also known as the Dragačevski Sabor (Serbian: Драгачевски сабор or Dragačevo Fair (Fete, Gathering or Assembly), pronounced [draɡǎːtʃeʋskiː sǎːbor]), is an annual Balkan brass band festival and competition held in the town of Guča, in the Dragačevo region and Lučani municipality of Serbia. The festival takes place from Friday to Sunday in early August and includes an opening concert on Friday night, celebrations on Saturday night and a competition on Sunday. The opening concert on Friday includes previous competition winners, each band getting to play three tunes while folk dancers in traditional dress dance kolos and oros. The song Sa Ovčara i Kablara marks the beginning of the festival each year. Apart from the regular brass orchestras, the competition also includes the children and youth orchestras.…

Name: Hadaka Matsuri
Best For: Cultural immersion, Traditional heritage, Adrenaline seekers
Description: Hadaka Matsuri (Naked Festival) at Saidaiji Temple in Okayama is one of Japan's strangest and most compelling events. Roughly 10,000 men wearing only white loincloths (fundoshi) in near-freezing temperatures compete to catch sacred wooden sticks (shingi) thrown by a priest at midnight. The belief is that whoever catches them will have a year of good fortune. The crush of bodies in the darkness is intense and genuinely chaotic. It has been held for over 500 years.

Name: Hay Festival Hay on Wye
Best For: Cultural immersion, Romantic getaway, Art and design
Description: The Hay Festival of Literature & Arts, or simply the Hay Festival (Welsh: Gŵyl Y Gelli), is an annual literature festival held in Hay-on-Wye, Powys, Wales, for 10 days from May to June. Devised by Norman, Rhoda and Peter Florence in 1988, the festival was described by Bill Clinton in 2001 as "The Woodstock of the mind". Tony Benn said: "In my mind it's replaced Christmas". It has become a prominent festival in British culture, and sessions at the festival have been recorded for television and radio programmes such as The Readers' and Writers' Roadshow and The One Show. All the BBC's national radio channels apart from BBC Radio 1 have been involved in broadcasting from the festival, and Sky Arts showed highlights of the festival from 2010 until 2013, handing over the main coverage to the BBC for the 2014 event.

Name: Hirosaki Cherry Blossom Festival
Description: Hirosaki Castle Park in Aomori Prefecture contains roughly 2,600 cherry trees and is considered by many to be Japan's finest cherry blossom viewing location. The moats fill with fallen pink petals creating a floating carpet effect (hanaikada) that is genuinely extraordinary. The castle backdrop, the perfectly maintained grounds, and the density of blossoms make Hirosaki a step above even Kyoto for blossom season. A full festival with food stalls and lantern illumination runs during peak bloom.

Name: Holi Festival Mathura
Description: Holi (IPA: ['hoːli:, hoːɭiː]) is a major Hindu festival of colours, love, and spring. It celebrates the love between the deities Radha and Krishna. Additionally, the day signifies the triumph of good over evil, as it commemorates the victory of Vishnu as Narasimha over Hiranyakashipu. Holi originated in ancient Indian subcontinent and is predominantly celebrated in the Indian subcontinent, but has also spread to other regions of Asia and parts of the Western world. Holi also celebrates the arrival of spring in India and Nepal, the end of winter, and the blossoming of love. It is also an invocation for a good spring harvest season. It lasts for a night and a day, starting on the evening of the Purnima (full moon day) falling on the Hindu calendar month of Phalguna, which falls around the middle of March in the Gregorian calendar.

Name: Holy Week Braga
Best For: Religious significance, Traditional heritage, Cultural immersion
Description: Holy Week (Koine Greek: Ἁγία καὶ Μεγάλη Ἑβδομάς, romanized: Hagía kaì Megálē Hebdomás, lit. 'Holy and Great Week') commemorates the seven days leading up to Easter. It begins with the commemoration of Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem on Palm Sunday, marks the betrayal of Jesus on Spy Wednesday (Holy Wednesday), climaxing with the commemoration of the Last Supper on Maundy Thursday (Holy Thursday) and the Passion of Jesus on Good Friday (Holy Friday). Holy Week concludes with Christ's death and descent into hell on Holy Saturday. For all Christian traditions, it is a moveable observance. In Eastern Christianity, which also calls it Great Week, it is the week following Great Lent and Lazarus Saturday, starting on the evening of Palm Sunday and concluding on the evening of Great Saturday.…

Name: Hungry Ghost Festival
Best For: Cultural immersion, Traditional heritage, Local authenticity
Description: The Hungry Ghost Festival (Zhong Yuan Jie) is a month-long Chinese festival during which the gates of the underworld are believed to open and spirits roam the earth. In Penang — which has the most elaborate Hungry Ghost Festival outside of Taiwan — entire streets burn enormous quantities of joss paper, incense, and elaborate paper offerings. Community stages (getai) host Hokkien opera and live entertainment for the ghosts. Walking through Penang's streets at night during the festival, with braziers burning and opera playing to empty seats reserved for spirits, is genuinely otherworldly.

Name: Hwacheon Sancheoneo Ice Festival
Description: Hwacheon Sancheoneo Ice Festival is centred on a single extraordinary activity: ice fishing for mountain trout (sancheoneo) on the frozen Hwacheon River. Hundreds of holes are drilled in the ice and visitors fish, then grill their catch on the riverbank. Snow sleds, ice soccer, and a full winter village of activities surround the main fishing. It is a profoundly Korean winter experience — locals bundled against the cold, grilling fish over gas burners on frozen ice, children on sleds. Around 1 million visitors attend over the festival's run.

Name: Infiorata di Noto
Best For: Photography, Art and design, Romantic getaway
Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Infiorata di Noto” automatically. Name: Jallikattu Bull Taming Festival
Best For: Cultural immersion, Adrenaline seekers, Traditional heritage
Description: Jallikattu is the ancient Tamil bull-taming sport of the Pongal harvest festival — a practice documented for over 2,500 years. Zebu bulls are released from a pen and participants must grab and hold the bull's hump for a prescribed distance to win. The bulls are bred for aggression and the event is genuinely dangerous; injuries to both participants and spectators are common. It was banned in 2017, reinstated after mass protests, and remains a symbol of Tamil cultural identity. The Alanganallur version near Madurai is the most famous.

Name: Jewish Culture Festival
Best For: Cultural immersion, Music lovers, Traditional heritage
Description: The Jewish Culture Festival in Kraków (Polish: Festiwal Kultury Żydowskiej w Krakowie, Yiddish: ייִדישער קולטור־פֿעסטיוואַל אין קראָקע) is an annual cultural event organized since 1988 in the once Jewish district of Kazimierz (part of Kraków) by the Jewish Culture Festival Society headed by Janusz Makuch, a self-described meshugeneh ("crazy person"), fascinated with all things Jewish. The main goal of the festival is to educate people about Jewish culture, history, and faith (Judaism), which flourished in Poland before the Holocaust, as well as to familiarize them with modern Jewish culture, developing mostly in the United States and Israel, and finally, to provide entertainment. Each festival is held in late June or early July and takes nine days, from Saturday to Sunday. During that time concerts, exhibitions, plays, lectures, workshops, tours, etc. are organized.…

Name: Jinju Lantern Festival
Description: The Jinju Namgang Yudeung Festival is one of Korea's most beautiful annual events. Thousands of handcrafted lotus and themed lanterns illuminate the Nam River for ten nights, with the Chokseongnu Pavilion providing a historic backdrop. The festival commemorates a battle against Japanese forces in 1592, when lanterns were used to signal troops across the river. Today, lantern processions, traditional performances, and international lantern design competitions fill the riverfront. The reflection of lanterns on the dark water is superb.

Name: Juhannus Helsinki
Best For: Summer travel, Romantic getaway, Traditional heritage
Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Juhannus Helsinki” automatically. If you send us a short, verifiable blurb (or a Wikipedia/Wikidata link), we’ll drop it in festival-content-manual.ts. Name: Kaziukas Fair
Best For: Food travel, Street celebrations, Winter travel
Description: Kaziuko mugė or Saint Casimir's Fair is a large annual folk arts and crafts fair in Lithuania, dating to the beginning of the 17th century. The fair is traditionally held in city's markets and streets on the Sunday nearest to 4 March (Feast of St. Casimir), the anniversary of Saint Casimir's death. In Lithuanian, Kaziukas is a diminutive of Casimir. Today, Saint Casimir's fair also features music, dance, theater performances; it attracts tens of thousands of visitors and many craftsmen from across Lithuania as well as from neighbouring countries such as Latvia, Estonia, and Poland. In recent years, the fair has expanded into other cities in Lithuania, Belarus, Poland.

Name: Kirkpinar Oil Wrestling Festival
Description: Kırkpınar (Turkish for "forty springs") is a Turkish oil wrestling (yağlı güreş) tournament where pehlivans (wrestlers) compete for three days. It is held annually, usually in late June, near Edirne, Turkey since 1360. In the finals held on the last day, the first, second and third winners of each category are determined. During the tournament, also the Kırkpınar Festival is organized. Oil wrestling is a sport at the forefront of traditional Turkish sports. Wrestling was held in all of the fairs organized all over the Ottoman Empire, but the wrestler who won the "chief" title only in Kırkpınar was known as the "chief wrestler" until the next year's Kırkpınar wrestling. This idea continues to this day.

Name: Kirkpinar Oil Wrestling Festival
Description: Kirkpinar is the oldest continuously running sporting event in the world — 660 years of unbroken annual oil wrestling in Edirne. Competitors (pehlivan) pour olive oil over their bodies and leather trousers and wrestle without weight classes until one man is pinned. The winner receives a golden belt. The ceremony, the rankings, and the traditions of the wrestlers have been essentially unchanged since the 14th century. UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. The combination of genuine athletic drama, extraordinary history, and the unusual spectacle of oil-slicked wrestling makes this a top-tier global Festivo entry.

Name: Kotor Carnival
Best For: Street celebrations, Romantic getaway, Winter travel
Description: Kotor Carnival includes winter masquerade parades and cultural events in Montenegro’s old coastal town, blending Adriatic carnival customs with local music and costume traditions. Events are concentrated in and around the historic center.

Name: Krakow Wianki Festival
Best For: Summer travel, Romantic getaway, Traditional heritage
Description: Wianki (Wreaths, in English) is a cyclical cultural event, taking place annually in Kraków at the bend of Wisła river, near the Wawel hill.

Name: Krampusnacht — Austria
Best For: Winter travel, Traditional heritage, Cultural immersion
Description: The Krampus (German: [ˈkʁampʊs]) is a horned anthropomorphic figure who, in the Central and Eastern Alpine folkloric tradition, is said to accompany Saint Nicholas on visits to children during the night of 5 December (Krampusnacht; "Krampus Night"), immediately before the Feast of St. Nicholas on 6 December. In this tradition, Saint Nicholas rewards well-behaved children with small gifts, while Krampus punishes badly behaved ones with birch rods. The origin of the figure is unclear; some folklorists and anthropologists have postulated that it may have pre-Christian origins, although historians generally consider this unlikely, as it is not attested until the 16th century. In certain traditional parades and in such events as the Krampuslauf ("Krampus run"), some young men dressed as Krampus attempt to scare the audience with their antics. Krampus is featured on holiday greeting cards called Krampuskarten.…

Name: Kurentovanje — Ptuj
Best For: Street celebrations, Winter travel, Traditional heritage
Description: Kurentovanje is Slovenia's most popular and ethnologically significant carnival event first organised in 1960 by Drago Hasl and his associates from cultural and educational organizations. This 11-day rite of spring and fertility highlight event is celebrated on Shrove Sunday in Ptuj, the oldest documented city in the region, and draws around 100,000 participants in total each year. In 2016 proclaimed as the 7th largest carnival in the world by Lonely Planet. Its main figure, known as Kurent or Korent, has been popularly (but incorrectly) reinterpreted as an extravagant god of unrestrained pleasure and hedonism in early Slavic customs. In today's festival, groups of kurents or kurenti wear traditional sheepskin garments while holding wooden clubs with hedgehog skins attached called ježevke, the noise of which is believed to "chase away winter".…

Name: Lajkonik Parade
Description: The Lajkonik is one of the unofficial symbols of the city of Kraków, Poland. It is represented as a bearded man resembling a Tatar in a characteristic pointed hat, dressed in Mongol attire, with a wooden horse around his waist (hobby horse). It is the subject of the Lajkonik Festival (Polish: Lajkoniki) that takes place each year on the first Thursday after the religious holiday of Corpus Christi.

Name: Lathmar Holi Barsana
Description: Lathmar Holi in Barsana (the village of Radha, consort of Krishna) is perhaps the most extraordinary single day in the Hindu festival calendar. Women from Barsana chase men from the neighbouring village of Nandgaon with wooden sticks (lathis), who must shield themselves while trying to apply colour. The ritual re-enacts the mythology of Krishna teasing Radha. The atmosphere — a dense crowd, clouds of coloured powder, bamboo sticks flying, dhol drums, and devotional singing — is overwhelming in the best way. One day only; one location.

Name: Lewes Bonfire Night
Description: Lewes Bonfire is a set of celebrations held in the town of Lewes in Sussex, England, and the United Kingdom's largest Bonfire Night festivities, with Lewes being called the bonfire capital of the world. Always held on 5 November (unless the 5th falls on a Sunday, in which case it is held on Saturday the 4th), the event not only marks Guy Fawkes Night – the date of the uncovering of the Gunpowder Plot in 1605 – but also commemorates the memory of the seventeen Protestant martyrs from the town burned at the stake for their faith during the Marian Persecutions. Lewes is part of the Sussex bonfire tradition. There are seven societies putting on six separate processions and firework displays throughout Lewes on 5 November. As well as this, 25–30 societies from all around Sussex come to Lewes on the fifth to march the streets.…

Name: Līgo — Riga
Best For: Traditional heritage, Summer travel, Romantic getaway
Description: Līgo (23 June) and Jāņi (24 June) are Latvia’s midsummer celebration: bonfires, singing, folk music, floral wreaths, and feasting—beer, cheese, and grilled food. Riga stages concerts and public events, while many families celebrate in yards and countryside. The holiday marks the summer solstice; English Wikipedia treats the tradition under the title Jāņi.

Name: Lopburi Monkey Banquet Festival
Description: The Lopburi Monkey Banquet is exactly what it sounds like — a feast prepared for the 3,000 long-tailed macaques that inhabit the ancient Khmer ruins of Phra Prang Sam Yot in the centre of Lopburi. Two tonnes of fruit, vegetables, and prepared food are laid out on decorated tables beside the ruins as an annual thank-you to the monkeys (who bring tourists and thereby prosperity). The monkeys climb over everything and everyone within reach. The event was started in 1989 by a local hotel owner and has become Lopburi's most famous annual event.

Name: Loy Krathong
Description: Loy Krathong at the ancient ruins of Sukhothai is considered the most atmospheric version of Thailand's festival of light. Thousands of candlelit krathong (lotus-shaped floats) are released onto the moats and lakes surrounding the 13th-century temples, while fire performances, traditional dance, and illuminated ruins create a setting of extraordinary beauty. Sukhothai is the birthplace of the festival and retains its spiritual character better than the tourist-heavy Chiang Mai version. The Historical Park becomes a floating garden of light.

Name: Maastricht Carnival
Best For: Street celebrations, Group trips, Winter travel
Description: Carnival (known as Shrovetide in certain localities) is a festive season that occurs at the close of the Christian pre-Lenten period, consisting of Quinquagesima or Shrove Sunday, Shrove Monday, and Shrove Tuesday or Mardi Gras. Carnival typically involves public celebrations, including events such as parades, public street parties and other entertainments, combining some elements of a circus. Elaborate costumes and masks allow people to set aside their everyday individuality and experience a heightened sense of social unity. Participants often indulge in excessive consumption of alcohol, meat, and other foods that will be forgone during upcoming Lent. Traditionally, butter, milk, and other animal products were not consumed "excessively", rather, their stock was fully consumed during Shrovetide as to reduce waste.…

Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Madonna della Bruna Matera” automatically. Name: Maslenitsa
Best For: Winter travel, Food travel, Traditional heritage
Description: Maslenitsa (Belarusian: Масленіца; Russian: Мaсленица [ˈmas⁽ʲ⁾lʲɪnʲɪt͡sə]; Rusyn: Пущаня; Ukrainian: Масниця), also known as Butter Lady, Butter Week, Crepe week, or Cheesefare Week, is an Eastern Slavic religious and folk holiday which has retained a number of elements of Slavic mythology in its ritual. It is celebrated during the last week before Great Lent; that is, the eighth week before Eastern Orthodox Pascha, equivalent to the West's Sexagesima. The date of Maslenitsa changes every year, depending on the date of the celebration of Easter. It corresponds to the Western Christian Carnival, except that Orthodox Lent begins on a Monday instead of a Wednesday, and the Orthodox date of Easter can differ greatly from the Western Christian date. The traditional attributes of the Maslenitsa celebration are the Maslenitsa effigy, sleigh rides, and festivities.…

Name: Maslenitsa Pancake Week
Best For: Cultural immersion, Traditional heritage, Food travel
Description: Maslenitsa (Butter Week / Pancake Week) is the Slavic pre-Lenten festival — a week of blini (pancakes), sledding, snow fortress battles, and the burning of a straw effigy representing winter. In Moscow's Gorky Park and Kolomenskoye estate, the festival is staged with traditional costumes, folk music, tug-of-war on ice, and enormous quantities of food. The burning of Lady Maslenitsa on the final Sunday — Forgiveness Sunday — is genuinely moving: a collective release before the austerity of Lent.

Best For: Traditional heritage, Food travel, Local authenticity
Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Mnarja Nadur Gozo” automatically. Name: Nadur Carnival Gozo
Description: The Nadur Carnival is unique among the many manifestations of Carnival. It is not organized by any committee and subsequently no rules apply. Sunset invites a multitude of masked and hooded creatures to throng the streets of Nadur - creatures wearing all kinds of funny and grotesque costumes. Nadur is a small locality with a population of 4500, on the island of Gozo - the sister island of Malta - in the middle of the Mediterranean about 50 km south of Sicily. It is run by a Local Council with 5 elected members: the Mayor, Deputy Mayor and 3 Councilors with one full-time Executive Secretary. Besides the religious feast of St. Peter & St. Paul, known locally as "L-Imnarja" held on 29 June and which lasts a whole week, Nadur is also famous for its annual revelry held on the 5 days preceding Ash Wednesday, the beginning of Lent.…

Name: Notte della Taranta
Best For: Music lovers, Traditional heritage, Summer travel
Description: Notte della Taranta ('Taranta Night') is a music festival in Salento, Italy. The Notte della Taranta is focused on pizzica, a popular folk genre in Salento, and takes place in various municipalities in the province of Lecce and the Grecìa Salentina, especially in Melpignano. It gives great importance to the folk music tradition of taranta and pizzica, and it is a resource for tourism in Apulia. The festival tours around Salento, normally culminating in a grand finale concert in Melpignano in August, which lasts until late night. An average of 120,000 spectators attend the last concert every year. The festival started in 1998 by an initiative of several municipalities of the Salento, which sponsored the event. Every year a new musical director is chosen. In 2015, Ludovico Einaudi released his album Taranta Project, inspired by his time directing the festival.

Name: Obby Oss Festival
Best For: Traditional heritage, Local authenticity, Street celebrations
Description: The 'Obby 'Oss festival () is a folk custom that takes place each 1st of May in Padstow, a coastal town in North Cornwall. It involves two separate processions making their way around the town, each containing an eponymous hobby horse known as the 'Obby 'Oss. The festival starts at midnight on the 30th of April when townspeople gather outside the Golden Lion Inn to sing the "Night Song". By morning, the town has been dressed with greenery and flowers placed around the maypole. The excitement begins with the appearance of one of the 'Obby 'Osses. Male dancers cavort through the town dressed as one of two 'Obby 'Osses, the "Old" and the "Blue Ribbon" or "Methodist" 'Obby 'Osses; as the name suggests, they are stylised depictions of horses. Prodded on by assistants known as "Teasers", each wears a mask and black frame-hung cape under which they try to catch young maidens as they pass through the town.…

Name: Olsok Trondheim
Best For: Religious significance, Traditional heritage, Cultural immersion
Description: Olsok (lit. 'Olaf's Wake' or 'Olaf's Vigil') is a national day of celebration on July 29 in the Nordic countries of Norway and the Faroe Islands, and also in the provinces of Härjedalen in Sweden and Savonlinna in Finland.

Name: Ommegang — Brussels
Description: The Ommegang of Brussels (French: Ommegang de Bruxelles; Dutch: Ommegang van Brussel) is a traditional Ommegang, a type of medieval pageant, held annually in Brussels, Belgium. It is celebrated for its elaborate display and deep cultural significance. Originating in the 14th century as Brussels' largest lustral procession honouring a miraculous statue of the Virgin Mary, the event gradually evolved into a civic spectacle involving all the city's constituents. Since 1930, it has taken the form of a historical reenactment of the Joyous Entry of Emperor Charles V and his son, Philip II, in the city in 1549. The colourful parade includes floats, traditional processional giants, and scores of folkloric groups, either on foot or on horseback, dressed in medieval garb. As such, it joins the wider tradition of processions of notable riders and giants found throughout Belgium and northern France.…

Name: Onam Festival
Best For: Cultural immersion, Traditional heritage, Food travel
Description: Onam is Kerala's most important harvest festival — a 10-day celebration of the mythical King Mahabali's annual return to his kingdom. The festival features the Vallamkali snake boat races on the Pampa river, spectacular pookalam floral carpets laid outside every home, Thiruvathirakali dance, and the elaborate Onasadya feast of 26 dishes served on banana leaves. The Thrissur Pooram (held earlier in the year) is often confused with Onam but both represent Kerala's extraordinary festival culture.

Name: Otaru Snow Light Path Festival
Description: The Otaru Snow Light Path Festival (Yuki Akari no Michi) lines the city's famous canal and side streets with thousands of hand-made snow lanterns and candles. Unlike Sapporo's grand ice sculptures, Otaru's festival is intimate — glowing lanterns reflected in the canal, jazz drifting from nearby bars, steam from hot drinks. The combination of Meiji-era stone warehouses, candlelit snow, and the quiet canal makes it one of Japan's most photogenic winter events. Often combined with a day trip from Sapporo (40 min by train).

Name: Patras Carnival (Apokries)
Description: Carnival (known as Shrovetide in certain localities) is a festive season that occurs at the close of the Christian pre-Lenten period, consisting of Quinquagesima or Shrove Sunday, Shrove Monday, and Shrove Tuesday or Mardi Gras. Carnival typically involves public celebrations, including events such as parades, public street parties and other entertainments, combining some elements of a circus. Elaborate costumes and masks allow people to set aside their everyday individuality and experience a heightened sense of social unity. Participants often indulge in excessive consumption of alcohol, meat, and other foods that will be forgone during upcoming Lent. Traditionally, butter, milk, and other animal products were not consumed "excessively", rather, their stock was fully consumed during Shrovetide as to reduce waste.…

Name: Perchtenlauf — Salzburg
Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Perchtenlauf — Salzburg” automatically. If you send us a short, verifiable blurb (or a Wikipedia/Wikidata link), we’ll drop it in festival-content-manual.ts. Name: Phuket Vegetarian Festival
Description: Phuket's Vegetarian Festival (Nine Emperor Gods Festival) is one of Southeast Asia's most extreme religious spectacles. Chinese Taoist devotees observe strict vegetarianism for nine days while spirit mediums (mah song) enter trance states and pierce their cheeks, tongues, and bodies with skewers, swords, and unlikely objects without apparent pain. The street processions of pierced devotees — surrounded by firecrackers, incense, and white-clad devotees — are entirely sincere religious rituals, not performances. The most intense version of this festival in Thailand.

Name: Pierogi Festival
Description: Pierogi ( pirr-OH-ghee; Polish: [pjɛˈrɔɡʲi] , sg. pieróg [ˈpjɛruk] ) are filled dumplings made by wrapping unleavened dough around a filling and cooking in boiling water. They are occasionally flavored with a savory or sweet garnish. Typical fillings include potato and twaróg cheese, sauerkraut, ground meat, mushrooms, fruits, or berries. Savory pierogi are often served with a topping of sour cream, fried onions, or both. Dumplings under the name pierogi are a traditional Polish dish, holding considerable culinary significance in Poland. The recipe itself dates back to at least 1682, when Poland's first cookbook, Compendium ferculorum, albo Zebranie potraw, was published. Pierogi are also popular in modern-day Canadian and American cuisines.

Name: Pingxi Sky Lantern Festival
Description: The Pingxi Sky Lantern Festival on the full moon of the first lunar month (Lantern Festival) is Taiwan's most iconic image: thousands of paper sky lanterns rising simultaneously from the narrow mountain valley town of Pingxi, wishes written in red on their sides, glowing against the dark sky. The effect of hundreds of lanterns ascending together, reflecting in the Jilong River, is genuinely moving. Multiple release events happen throughout the day and evening; the official midnight release coordinated by Taiwan's Tourism Bureau is the most dramatic.

Name: Pohoda Festival
Best For: Music lovers, Summer travel, Group trips
Description: The word "pohoda" means "relax" or "ease" in the Slovak and Czech languages. Pohoda is an open-air summer music festival in Slovakia, first organized in 1997 in Trenčín. It is the biggest Slovak music event organized annually. The current venue is Trenčín Airport. In addition to a variety of musical genres (rock, pop, indie, electronic, classical, experimental and folk), its programme is full of contemporary art – from visual art, theatre, literature, film, dance to stand-up comedy and various workshops, discussions and a zone of non-profit organizations. Pohoda has been held at the Trenčín Airport since 2004, and the current maximum capacity of the festival is 30,000 visitors. The festival's programme takes place on 16 stages, which each year host over 130 musical performers from all over the world.…

Name: Pushkar Camel Fair
Description: The Pushkar Camel Fair is one of the world's largest camel fairs and one of India's most visually extraordinary events. Over 50,000 camels, horses, and cattle are traded over five days on the desert dunes outside Pushkar, alongside a full cultural festival and the sacred Kartik Purnima bathing festival on the holy lake. Hot air balloon rides over the fair at dawn, camel races, turban-tying competitions, and an endless photographic feast of decorated animals and Rajasthani dress. The combination of commerce, religion, and spectacle is unlike anywhere else on earth.

Name: Rann Utsav
Description: Rann Utsav is a three-month festival on the white salt desert of the Rann of Kutch in Gujarat — the largest salt flat in the world. During the full moon, the desert glows silver-white under the moonlight, an almost surreal landscape. The festival includes cultural performances, Gujarati handicrafts, traditional music, local food, and adventure activities including camel rides and ATV tours across the salt flat. Gujarat government runs a large tent city (Dhordo) as the festival hub.

Name: Rijeka Carnival
Description: The Rijeka Carnival (Croatian: Riječki karneval) is held each year before Lent (between late January and early March) in Rijeka, West Croatia. Established in 1982, it has become the biggest carnival in Croatia.

Name: Romaria de Nossa Senhora d'Agonia — Viana do Castelo
Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Romaria de Nossa Senhora d'Agonia — Viana do Castelo” automatically. Name: Santa Marija Feast Victoria Gozo
Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Santa Marija Feast Victoria Gozo” automatically. If you send us a short, verifiable blurb (or a Wikipedia/Wikidata link), we’ll drop it in festival-content-manual.ts. For now: this event is associated with Victoria, Malta, around your listed dates—double-check schedules with organizers.

Name: Sarajevo Film Festival
Best For: Art and design, Cultural immersion, Photography
Description: The Sarajevo Film Festival is the premier and largest film festival in Southeast Europe, and is one of the largest film festivals in Europe. It was founded in Sarajevo in 1995 during the siege of Sarajevo in the Bosnian War, and brings international and local celebrities to Sarajevo every year. It is held in August and showcases an extensive variety of feature and short films from around the world. The current director of the festival is Jovan Marjanović.

Name: Sardine Festival
Best For: Food travel, Summer travel, Street celebrations
Description: The Sardine Festival in Portimão is a summer waterfront event built around grilled sardines, local food stalls, and evening music programming along the riverfront. It typically runs multiple nights during peak holiday season.

Name: Sechseläuten — Zürich
Best For: Traditional heritage, Weekend getaway, Cultural immersion
Description: The Sechseläuten (Zurich German: Sächsilüüte, "The six o'clock ringing of the bells") is a traditional spring holiday in the Swiss city of Zurich celebrated in its current form, usually on the 3rd Monday of April, since the early 20th century.

Name: Sighisoara Medieval Festival
Description: Sighişoara Medieval Festival or Festivalul Sighişoara medievală is a Romanian festival held on the last weekend of July in Sighişoara, Romania.

Name: Song and Dance Celebration
Best For: Cultural immersion, Traditional heritage, Summer travel
Description: Estonia’s Song and Dance Celebration in Tallinn is one of the country’s signature cultural events, bringing together mass choirs, folk dance groups, and national costume traditions in a multi-day program. The largest performances are centered on the Tallinn Song Festival Grounds and major city venues, with participants arriving from across Estonia and diaspora communities.

Name: Swedish Midsummer
Best For: Summer travel, Traditional heritage, Romantic getaway
Description: Midsummer or Midsommar (Swedish: [ˈmɪ̌ˌsɔmːar] or [ˈmǐːdˌsɔmːar]) is a celebration of the season of summer, taking place on or near the date of the summer solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, the longest day of the year. The name midsummer mainly refers to summer solstice festivals of European origin. These cultures traditionally regard it as the middle of summer, with the season beginning on May Day. Although the summer solstice falls on 20, 21 or 22 June in the Northern Hemisphere, it was traditionally reckoned to fall on 23–24 June in much of Europe. These dates were Christianized as Saint John's Eve and Saint John's Day. It is usually celebrated with outdoor gatherings that include bonfires, maypole dancing and feasting.

Name: Tallinn Medieval Days
Description: Tallinn Medieval Days (Medieval Days / similar branding by year) is a summer heritage event concentrated in the UNESCO-listed Old Town: costumed performers, crafts, music, and market stalls play against the Hanseatic streetscape. Programming shifts annually but usually runs for several days in high season. Treat it as a pedestrian, family-friendly street festival layered onto normal tourism in an already crowded historic core.

Name: Tamborrada San Sebastian
Description: Tamborrada of Donostia (Basque: Donostiako Danborrada) is a celebratory drum festival held every year on January 20 in the city of San Sebastián, Spain. At midnight, in the Konstituzio Plaza in the "Alde Zaharra/Parte Vieja" (Old Town), the mayor raises the flag of San Sebastián. The festival lasts for 24 hours. Participants, dressed as cooks and soldiers, march in companies across the city. The celebration ends at midnight, when people congregate at the Konstituzio Plaza and the city flag is simultaneously lowered at various locations.

Name: Tbilisoba
Best For: Food travel, Street celebrations, Cultural immersion
Description: Tbilisoba (Georgian: თბილისობა) is an annual October festival which celebrates the diversity and history of Tbilisi, the capital of Georgia. It was first held on October 28, 1979, and has since become an established tradition. The festival features open-air concerts of traditional music and dancing and various cultural events, centered on Old Tbilisi, the historical part of the city. Beyond celebrating the city's past and present, people from all over Georgia represent their region at the fair of the harvest and Rtveli. Awarding honorary citizenship of Tbilisi by the city government also occurs in the framework of Tbilisoba.

Name: Thaipusam
Description: Thaipusam in Penang is one of the most intense religious festivals on earth. Tamil Hindu devotees carry kavadi — elaborate steel frames pierced through their skin, cheeks, and tongue — in fulfilment of vows to Lord Murugan. The procession from Sri Mahamariamman Temple to Arulmigu Balathandayuthapani Temple on Penang Hill is accompanied by drumming, chanting, and enormous crowds. The devotees enter a trance state and appear to feel no pain. Penang's version is particularly concentrated and accessible compared to Kuala Lumpur's Batu Caves version.

Name: Tihar Festival of Lights
Description: Tihar (Deepawali in Nepal) is Nepal's five-day festival of lights — each day dedicated to a different creature or deity: crows, dogs, cows, oxen, and finally the self (Bhai Tika). Dogs are garlanded with marigolds and given tikka marks on their foreheads on the second day — an extraordinary sight that reflects Hinduism's reverence for the animal world. On the third day (Laxmi Puja), Kathmandu and the Kathmandu Valley illuminate completely with oil lamps, candles, and electric lights. The Newari community celebrates with a separate night festival called Mha Puja.

Name: Torres Vedras Carnival
Description: Carnival (known as Shrovetide in certain localities) is a festive season that occurs at the close of the Christian pre-Lenten period, consisting of Quinquagesima or Shrove Sunday, Shrove Monday, and Shrove Tuesday or Mardi Gras. Carnival typically involves public celebrations, including events such as parades, public street parties and other entertainments, combining some elements of a circus. Elaborate costumes and masks allow people to set aside their everyday individuality and experience a heightened sense of social unity. Participants often indulge in excessive consumption of alcohol, meat, and other foods that will be forgone during upcoming Lent. Traditionally, butter, milk, and other animal products were not consumed "excessively", rather, their stock was fully consumed during Shrovetide as to reduce waste.…

Name: Transhumance Provence
Description: Transhumance is a type of pastoralism or nomadism, a seasonal movement of livestock between fixed summer and winter pastures. In montane regions (vertical transhumance), it implies movement between higher pastures in summer and lower valleys in winter. Herders have a permanent home, typically in valleys. Generally only the herds travel, with a certain number of people necessary to tend them, while the main population stays at the base. In contrast, movement in plains or plateaus (horizontal transhumance) is more susceptible to disruption by climatic, economic, or political change. Traditional or fixed transhumance has occurred throughout the inhabited world, particularly Europe and western Asia. It is often important to pastoralist societies, as the dairy products of transhumance flocks and herds (milk, butter, yogurt and cheese) may form much of the diet of such populations.…

Name: Up Helly Aa — Lerwick
Best For: Fire ceremonies, Heritage travel, Remote destinations
Description: The Lerwick Up Helly Aa is the largest Up Helly Aa fire festival celebrated annually on the last Tuesday in January in Lerwick, the capital of Shetland, Scotland. A Guizer Jarl has featured in each Lerwick Up Helly Aa since the introduction of the position in 1906. The Guizer Jarl is the chief guizer, who (in the modern festival) leads a squad dressed as Vikings who are the primary focus of the festival's proceedings. Only the Jarl's Squad dresses as Vikings; the festival's other squads dress up in other costumes associated with the act they perform throughout the halls opened after the torchlight procession. It took until after the First World War until a squad of Vikings appeared in the festival every year. As the festival developed, each Guizer Jarl would select a name for the galley, which is constructed from October the previous year and burned at the culmination of the torchlight procession.…

Name: Uzgavenes Vilnius
Best For: Street celebrations, Traditional heritage, Winter travel
Description: Užgavėnės [ʊʒ'ɡaːvʲeːnʲeːs] is an ancient Lithuanian festival that takes place during the seventh week before Easter (Ash Wednesday). The celebration corresponds to Roman Catholic holiday traditions in other parts of the world, such as Mardi Gras, Shrove Tuesday, and Carnaval. Užgavėnės begins on the night before Ash Wednesday, when an effigy of winter (usually named Morė) is burnt. A major element of the holiday, meant to symbolize the defeat of winter in the Northern Hemisphere, is a staged battle between Lašininis ("porky") personifying winter and Kanapinis ("hempen man") personifying spring. Devils, witches, goats, the grim reaper, and other joyful and frightening characters appear in costumes during the celebrations. The participants and masqueraders dance and eat the traditional dish of the holiday - pancakes with a variety of toppings.…

Name: Victory Day Grand Harbour Regatta
Description: Victory Day (Maltese: Jum il-Vitorja) is a public holiday celebrated in Malta on 8 September and recalls the end of three historical sieges made on the Maltese archipelago – the Great Siege of Malta by the Ottoman Empire ending in 1565; the Siege of Valletta by the French Blockade ending in 1800; and the Siege of Malta during the Second World War by the Axis forces ending in 1943. The day also coincides with the commemoration of the birth of the Virgin Mary, better known as the Nativity of Mary, which is celebrated in the villages of Senglea, Naxxar and Mellieħa on the island of Malta, and Xagħra in Gozo; and the death of Queen Elizabeth II, the last colonial-era Maltese head of state. It is locally known as il-Vitorja (the Victory), il-Bambina (the Baby), and Otto Settembre (Italian for "8 September"). The traditional regatta featuring boat races in the Grand Harbour is held on Victory Day.

Name: Wacken Open Air
Best For: Music lovers, Adrenaline seekers, Group trips
Description: Wacken Open Air (, abbreviated as W:O:A) is a heavy metal music festival, held yearly since 1990 on the first weekend of August in the village of Wacken in Schleswig-Holstein, Germany. It is often called the world's biggest and most renowned metal festival, or the "Mecca of heavy metal". The four-day event involves around 200 bands, is attended by around 85,000 metalheads from more than 80 countries, and takes place on a 240-hectare site including campgrounds. Wacken has two main stages and several smaller stages, as well as areas with stalls, beer tents and entertainment; including a medieval-themed 'Wackinger' village and apocalyptic-themed 'Wasteland'. Wacken Open Air has long focused on traditional heavy metal and extreme metal genres such as power metal, speed metal, thrash metal, death metal, black metal, doom metal, industrial, symphonic and folk metal.…

Name: Walpurgisnacht Harz
Description: Walpurgis Night (), an abbreviation of Saint Walpurgis Night (from the German Sankt-Walpurgisnacht [zaŋkt valˈpʊʁɡɪsˌnaxt]), also known as Saint Walpurga's Eve (alternatively spelled Saint Walburga's Eve) and Walpurgisnacht, is the eve of the Christian feast day of Saint Walpurga, an 8th-century abbess in Francia, and is celebrated on the night of 30 April and the day of 1 May. This feast commemorates the canonization of Saint Walpurga and the movement of her relics to Eichstätt, both of which occurred on 1 May 870. Saint Walpurga was hailed by the Christians of Germany for battling "pest, rabies, and whooping cough, as well as against witchcraft". Christians prayed to God through the intercession of Saint Walpurga in order to protect themselves from witchcraft, as Saint Walpurga was successful in converting the local populace to Christianity.…

Name: Wife Carrying Championships
Best For: Adrenaline seekers, Summer travel, Group trips
Description: Wife carrying (Finnish: eukonkanto or akankanto) is a contest in which competitors race while each carrying a partner. Whilst most teams consist of a man carrying a woman there are generally no rules about gender or marital status. The objective is for the runner to carry their team-mate through a special obstacle track in the fastest time. The sport was first introduced in 1992 in Sonkajärvi, Finland. Several types of carrying may be practised: either a classic piggyback, a fireman's carry (over the shoulder), or Estonian-style ("wife" upside-down on his back with their legs over the neck and shoulders).

Name: Yosakoi Festival
Description: Yosakoi in Kochi is the parent festival of the dance form that has since spread across Japan. Around 20,000 dancers in elaborate costumes perform choreographed routines through the city streets and in ten designated stages, wielding naruko (wooden clappers). Each team of up to 150 dancers creates its own costume, music mix, and routine — judged on creativity and energy. The atmosphere is electric and the production quality of team costumes rivals major theatrical productions.

Name: Zejtun St Gregory Feast
Best For: Traditional heritage, Summer travel, Street celebrations
Description: Pope Gregory I (Latin: Gregorius I; Gregorio I; c. 540 – 12 March 604), commonly known as Saint Gregory the Great (Latin: Sanctus Gregorius Magnus; Italian: San Gregorio Magno), was the 64th Bishop of Rome from 3 September 590 until his death on 12 March 604. A Roman senator's son, and himself the prefect of Rome at 30, Gregory lived in a monastery that he established on his family estate before becoming a papal ambassador and then pope. During his papacy, his administration greatly surpassed that of the emperors Maurice and Theodosius in improving the welfare of the people of Rome. Gregory regained papal authority in Spain and France and sent missionaries to England, including Augustine of Canterbury and Paulinus of York. The realignment of barbarian allegiance to Rome from their Arian Christian alliances shaped medieval Europe. Gregory saw Franks, Lombards, and Visigoths align with Rome in religion.…

Name: Zundert Flower Parade
Best For: Photography, Art and design, Traditional heritage
Description: A flower parade is a parade in which the floats, vehicles, boats, participants, animals and other things are decorated or covered in flowers. Often there are other elements like marching bands and people in costumes. Flower parades are held in several countries, many of which celebrate the forthcoming of the seasons. The oldest flower parade dates back to the 1800s. The culture of flower and fruit parades in the Netherlands is inscribed with the International Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity of Unesco.

Name: Andong Mask Dance Festival
Best For: Cultural immersion, Traditional heritage, Arts and design
Description: The Andong International Mask Dance Festival is Korea's premier showcase of the hahoetal mask dance tradition — satirical masked performances that date to the Joseon Dynasty. Teams from Korea and over 20 countries perform traditional mask dances from their own cultures, creating a genuine world festival of a specific art form. Andong's Hahoe Folk Village (UNESCO-listed) provides the most atmospheric backdrop. The performances use exaggerated masks to satirise aristocracy, clergy, and social hierarchy — subversive humour encoded in performance.

Name: Ati-Atihan Festival
Description: Ati-Atihan in Kalibo, Aklan is the Philippines' most riotous festival and a direct ancestor of Rio-style street carnival. Participants cover their faces and bodies in soot or black paint in homage to the indigenous Ati people, then dance through the streets for days in elaborate costumes to thunderous tribal drumming. The festival honours the Santo Niño (child Jesus) but the African-inspired energy and visual intensity are unlike anything else in the Philippines. Called 'the mother of all Philippine festivals'.

Name: Baalbek International Festival
Best For: Arts and design, Cultural immersion, Traditional heritage
Description: Baalbek International Festival stages classical music, opera, ballet, and world music concerts inside the 2,000-year-old Roman temple complex of Baalbek — the most intact Roman ruins in the Middle East. The Temple of Bacchus serves as backstage; the Great Court of the Temple of Jupiter becomes the main stage. Artists including Fairouz, Ella Fitzgerald, Herbert von Karajan, and Miles Davis have performed here. Check Lebanon travel advisories before planning.

Name: Battle of the Oranges
Best For: Adrenaline seekers, Cultural immersion, Group trips
Description: The Battle of the Oranges in Ivrea, Piedmont is Europe's largest food fight — and one of its oldest. During the three days of carnival, nine teams on foot face down horse-drawn carts carrying 'aranceri' (orange throwers) in a re-enactment of a medieval uprising. Over 500 tonnes of oranges are used. Participation requires joining one of the nine recognised foot teams; wearing a red cap signals non-combat status. Orange juice flows ankle-deep in the streets; the stinging impact of a direct orange hit at speed is not to be underestimated.

Name: Carnival of Oruro
Description: Carnival (known as Shrovetide in certain localities) is a festive season that occurs at the close of the Christian pre-Lenten period, consisting of Quinquagesima or Shrove Sunday, Shrove Monday, and Shrove Tuesday or Mardi Gras. Carnival typically involves public celebrations, including events such as parades, public street parties and other entertainments, combining some elements of a circus. Elaborate costumes and masks allow people to set aside their everyday individuality and experience a heightened sense of social unity. Participants often indulge in excessive consumption of alcohol, meat, and other foods that will be forgone during upcoming Lent. Traditionally, butter, milk, and other animal products were not consumed "excessively", rather, their stock was fully consumed during Shrovetide as to reduce waste.…

Name: Chaharshanbe Suri Fire Jumping
Best For: Cultural immersion, Adrenaline seekers, Traditional heritage
Description: Chaharshanbe Suri (Red Wednesday) is the fire festival on the last Tuesday night before Persian New Year — one of the most dramatic participatory festivals in the world. Bonfires are lit in streets and parks across Iran; people leap over the flames chanting 'Give me your red colour, take back my pale sickly colour' — asking the fire for health and energy for the coming year. In Tehran, hundreds of simultaneous bonfires are lit in residential streets across the city. Fireworks, firecrackers, and the smell of smoke fill the air.

Name: Cooper's Hill Cheese-Rolling
Best For: Adrenaline seekers, Cultural immersion, Local authenticity
Description: Cooper's Hill Cheese-Rolling is one of Britain's most violent unofficial festivals. A 7–8 pound Double Gloucester cheese is rolled down a near-vertical hill (gradient up to 1:1) and competitors chase it — often tumbling, rolling, and colliding all the way down. St John Ambulance crews wait at the bottom. The winner keeps the cheese; the prize is otherwise entirely the glory. The event is technically unsanctioned and has no official organiser. It has been held for at least 200 years and possibly traces back to pagan spring rituals. Injuries (including serious ones) are annual.

Name: Crayfish Parties
Best For: Food travel, Summer travel, Local authenticity
Description: A crayfish party (Swedish: kräftskiva [ˈkrɛ̂ftˌɧiːva]) is a traditional summertime eating and drinking celebration in the Nordic countries. The tradition, originating in Sweden, has also spread to Finland via its Swedish-speaking population and Norway. A similar tradition exists in the Baltic countries, in particular in Lithuania and Latvia. Crayfish parties are generally held during August, a tradition that began because the crayfish harvest in Sweden was, for most of the 20th century, legally limited to the late summer. Nowadays, the kräftpremiär date in early August has no legal significance. Customary party accessories are novelty paper hats, paper tablecloths, paper lanterns (often depicting the Man in the Moon), and bibs. Akvavit and other kinds of snaps are served, as well as beer, and traditional drinking songs (snapsvisa) may be sung.…

Name: El Colacho
Description: El Colacho in Castrillo de Murcia is a Corpus Christi-weekend ritual where costumed participants leap over infants laid on mattresses as part of a long-running local tradition. The event combines religious procession elements with tightly managed ceremonial street performance.

Name: Festa San Filep Zebbug Gozo
Best For: Traditional heritage, Religious significance, Local authenticity
Description: Żebbuġ on Gozo marks the feast of Saint Philip (San Filep) with a traditional village festa: church services, processions, band marches, and fireworks over several days building to the feast. Visit Malta lists the feast on 14 June (confirm the annual programme for band marches and fireworks nights).

Name: Fiesta Santa Marta de Ribarteme
Best For: Religious significance, Traditional heritage, Cultural immersion
Description: We couldn’t match a solid English Wikipedia entry for “Fiesta Santa Marta de Ribarteme” automatically. If you send us a short, verifiable blurb (or a Wikipedia/Wikidata link), we’ll drop it in festival-content-manual.ts.
